text: This is All Things Considered from NPR News, I'm Lauren Fraer.
President Trump has made clear that the U.S. operation to capture Venezuelan President
Nicolas Maduro is, at least in part, about controlling Venezuela's oil. Trump said
yesterday at a press conference that U.S. oil companies will spend billions of dollars
to support the country's oil infrastructure. NPR's Julia Simon joins us now, and Julia,
give us a lay of the land. How big of a deal is Venezuela's oil?
Well Venezuela has one of the biggest oil resources in the world, but their oil output
has fallen a lot in recent decades. Venezuela once produced 3 million barrels a day, now
it's more like 1 million barrels. In the past, much of Venezuela's oil went to refineries
in the U.S., now much of it goes to China. And Lauren, it's important to remember that
all oil is bad for global warming. Burning it releases planet-heating carbon dioxide.
But Venezuela's oil, experts say, is among the worst for global warming. It requires
a lot of energy to extract, and it releases a lot of methane, another powerful pollutant.
And President Trump has said that Venezuela has stolen U.S. oil investment. What is he
referring to? Right. U.S. oil companies, they've been drilling
in Venezuela for more than a century. But around 2007, then President Hugo Chavez,
he really shook things up. Here's Francisco Menaldi, director of the Latin America Energy
Program at Rice University. So Chavez basically forcefully renegotiated
contracts. There were three major American companies, and two decided to leave and go
into international arbitration, and the other one decided to stay.
So Exxon Mobil and ConocoPhillips, they left and took the Venezuelan government to international
courts, which ordered that Venezuela pay ConocoPhillips over $10 billion and Exxon Mobil over $1 billion.
Venezuela's only paid a fraction of those sums. Meanwhile, the U.S. company that stayed
is Chevron. Trump has said that U.S. oil companies will
come into Venezuela. How likely is that, though?
Well, one positive thing for oil companies is that Venezuela is pretty well established.
Oil companies have a fairly good idea of what they will find when they drill. Menaldi says
for companies owed billions by the Venezuelan government, returning to the country could
be an opportunity to recoup some of that money. But look, Lauren, there's lots of reasons
oil companies would not want to come back. Yeah, I mean, I imagine some oil companies
might feel bruised by their past experiences there.
100%. And the global oil market, it faces an oversupply. The price of oil is pretty
low. Long-term projections for oil demand are unclear as the world shifts to more electric
vehicles. And it's still unclear who is in charge of Venezuela. No one's going to start
investing until they're sure their oil contracts are valid.
Julia, are there even broader regional implications for oil?
Yeah, people should also know that next door to Venezuela is Guyana. That's a country
which recently discovered massive amounts of oil. It's seen as one of the most attractive
places in the world for oil. ExxonMobil is there. For years, Venezuela and Guyana have
had a territorial conflict. It relates to oil rights. Last March, Venezuelan vessels
entered Guyana's territorial waters. And oil analysts tell NPR that, at least right
now, without Maduro, Guyana should feel more secure. By the way, ExxonMobil did not respond
to a request for comment.
That's NPR's Julia Simon. Julia, thank you.
